hero_summary: Tarz Jadid is a maqam whose description differs across schools. In some modern Arabic references it is read as a branch of the Nahawand family, while classical Turkish theory describes Tarz-ı Cedid as an independent composite maqam ending on Ajam Ashiran (low E / E tonic).
short_identity: In concise Arabic reading: Nahawand on Nawa (G) with Hijaz on Muhayyar (upper D). In the broader Turkish reading: an independent composite maqam with its final tonic on Ajam Ashiran (low E / E tonic).

quick_facts.karar: In the concise Arabic reading, the ear centers on Nawa (G); in Turkish theory, however, its final tonic is Ajam Ashiran (low E / E tonic)
quick_facts.ghammaz: Nawa (G) as the principal degree of strength, with possible suspension points on Rast (C) and Dukah (D)
quick_facts.lower_jins: In Arabic formulation: Nahawand on Nawa (G). In Turkish formulation: Busalik/Rast on Nawa, then a layer of Nahawand or Nev'eser on Rast (C)
quick_facts.upper_jins: In Arabic formulation: Hijaz on Muhayyar (upper D). In Turkish theory, however, the construction is broader than a single upper jins alone
quick_facts.common_start: It often begins around Nawa (G) with the power established there, then turns to the upper structure before descending gradually toward the final cadence
quick_facts.character: A maqam disputed between schools; it should not be reduced to Nahawand plus Hijaz only if one wishes to represent its Ottoman composite personality

sayr.summary: The practical sayr of Tarz Jadid differs according to the school one follows. In the concise Arabic reading, it begins in the field of Nahawand on Nawa (G), then brings out Hijaz on Muhayyar (upper D) in the jawab before returning to the center of repose. In the classical Turkish reading, however, its sayr is descending or descending-ascending: it begins around Nawa (G) with Busalik or Rast there, then passes through a layer of Nahawand or Nev'eser on Rast (C), and finally descends to Ajam Ashiran (low E / E tonic), where the complete cadence takes place through a Jaharkah-type closure on the tonic.
resting_tones: Nawa (G): the principal degree of strength and the half-cadential center in the Turkish description, and also the upper auditory center in the concise Arabic reading
Ajam Ashiran (low E / E tonic): the final tonic in classical Turkish theory
Rast (C) and Dukah (D): important stations of suspension and passage in the middle layers of the maqam
Muhayyar (upper D): an important upper location in the concise Arabic formulation because it carries the Hijaz of the jawab
motion_notes: This maqam is one of the places where the disagreement between Arabic and Turkish schools should be stated explicitly
The common Arabic shorthand reduces it to: Nahawand on Nawa (G) plus Hijaz on Muhayyar (upper D)
The Turkish school does not treat it as a mere Nahawand branch, but as an independent composite maqam with a specific tonic on Ajam Ashiran
Establishing Nawa (G) and then descending convincingly to Ajam Ashiran is an essential part of its Ottoman personality
If it is performed as though it were only a simple Nahawand, its composite identity is lost
common_path: Begin around Nawa (G) and establish the principal degree of strength there
Show Busalik or Rast on Nawa according to treatment
Pass through the layer of Rast (C) colored by Nahawand or Nev'eser
Bring out Hijaz on Muhayyar in the concise Arabic reading when needed
Descend to Ajam Ashiran (low E / E tonic) for the final closure
common_modulations: The Arabic reading places it within the Nahawand family because the upper structure is read through Nahawand on Nawa
The Turkish reading takes it out of being a mere Nahawand derivative and treats it as an independent composite maqam named Tarz-ı Cedid
Nev'eser may enter as part of its route in the Turkish conception, but it is not identical with the maqam itself
It differs from Nahawand Kabir because Nahawand Kabir belongs in Turkish classification to another route and another closure, whereas Tarz Jadid ends on Ajam Ashiran
It differs from Nahawand Murassa because Tarz Jadid is not simply a passing alteration in the upper jins, but a multi-layered construction in its Ottoman description
examples: İltifatınla gönül şad olduğu demdir bu dem
